does: This paper develops a unified mathematical framework for understanding concept alignment across different neural network models and modalities. The authors decompose alignment problems along two fundamental dimensions: what gets aligned (representations or concepts) and at what granularity (instance-wise or distributional levels). This decomposition yields four distinct alignment properties—instance-wise and distributional variants of translation consistency and concept consistency. The paper introduces InterVenchA, an intervention-based benchmark designed to separately measure extraction quality, translation quality, and concept consistency. Through theoretical analysis and empirical evaluation, the work demonstrates that commonly assumed equivalences between different alignment objectives break down in practice: optimizing for one property does not reliably guarantee recovery of others, and purely unsupervised approaches frequently fail to recover meaningful concept structures.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research examining structural similarities in learned representations across models, including work on neural network similarity metrics, representation alignment, and the study of emergent concepts in deep learning. It extends prior concept-based interpretability methods and representational similarity analysis by providing formal definitions where previous work used inconsistent or overlapping terminology. The framework responds to confusion in the literature where different methods claimed to solve alignment problems despite optimizing distinct objectives. It incorporates insights from intervention-based causal analysis and connects to broader efforts in mechanistic interpretability and understanding what features neural networks learn across different architectures and training procedures.
enables: This framework enables more rigorous future work on representation alignment by providing clear definitions and measurement tools. It opens directions for developing new alignment methods that explicitly optimize for combinations of the four identified properties rather than conflating them. The decomposition allows researchers to strategically choose which alignment properties matter for their application—whether instance-level correspondence or statistical similarity is primary. The intervention-based benchmark establishes methodology for cleanly measuring extraction and translation quality separately, facilitating better-designed concept discovery algorithms. This work also enables principled investigation of why certain alignment objectives fail, potentially revealing fundamental constraints on what can be aligned unsupervised versus what requires supervision or additional structural assumptions.
